content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Elizabeth G. Clark 
Sr. Deputy City Attorney 
City of Torrance 
3031 Torrance Boulevard 
Torrance, 
CA 
90503 
Dear Ms. Clark: 
October 7, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. I-88-352 
You have requested advice regarding the mass mailing 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")!! as 
recently amended by proposition 73. 
As city attorney for the 
City of Torrance, you have responsibility for advising the city 
about distribution of a quarterly publication known as the 
"Pipeline." 
This letter confirms telephone advice I rendered 
in response to your letter on September 20, 1988. 
QUESTION 
May the City of Torrance list elected officials in its 
quarterly newsletter? 
CONCLUSION 
The City of Torrance may include a roster listing of 
elected officers in its newsletter so long as the names of the 
elected officers are in the same type size and typeface as 
others listed. 
!! Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code 
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 


Elizabeth G. Clark 
October 7, 1988 
Page -2-
FACTS 
The city of Torrance distributes a quarterly newsletter 
to current city employees, retired employees and elected city 
officials. 
The newsletter, known as the "Pipeline," contains a 
standard roster-type listing,of elected officials. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act as amended by proposition 73 now provides that no 
mass mailing shall be sent at public expense. 
(Section 
89001.) 
The Commission has determined that the intent of the 
voters in adopting Proposition 73 was to prevent elected 
officers from gaining an advantage from incumbency by using 
public funds to send out newsletters and mass mailings which 
increase their name recognition. 
(See Regulation 18901, 
adopted as an emergency regulation, filed August 8, 1988; and 
Raye, et ale Advice Letters, No. A-88-220, copies enclosed.) 
Regulation 18901 provides that certain types of mailings 
are not prohibited by Section 89001. 
Subdivision (b) provides: 
(b) 
A mailing will not be deemed to be 
prohibited by Section 89001 if the mailing meets all 
of the following criteria: 
(1) 
It is mailed by an elected officer's 
agency; 
(2) 
The stationery, forms and envelopes used 
for the mailing are the official stationery, forms 
and envelopes of the agency; and 
(3) 
The elected officer's name appears, if at 
all, onl~ on the standard letterhead or logotype of 
the stat10nery, forms or envelopes and there are no 
other references to the officer, including his or 
her photograph or signature, in the mailing. 
(Emphasis added.) 
The regulation is intended to make clear that a 
mailing is not prohibited merely because it contains a 
roster listing of the agency's elected officials in its 
publication. 
This is consistent with our previous advice 
stating that a newsletter may list the members of 
governing boards and other agency officers in a standard 
roster-type listing, so long as the names of elected 
officers are in the same type size and typeface as others 
listed. 
(Raye Advice Letter, supra.) 
Proposed permanent 
Regulation 18901 would state this exception more 
explicitly. 
The Commission will consider adoption of the 
proposed permanent regulation on December 6, 1988. 


Elizabeth G. Clark 
October 7, 1988 
Page -3-
If you have any further questions, please contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
JGM: ld 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
By: 
John G. McLean 
Counsel, Legal Division 


STANLEY E. REMELMEYER 
CfTY ATTORNEY 
CITY Or: TORRAN 
3031 TORRANCE BOULEVARD, TORRANCE, CALIFORNIA 
TELEPHONe (213) 32S-5310 
September 2, 1988 
Fair Political Practices commission 
Conflicts Division 
428 "J" Street 
Suite 800 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
The city of Torrance distributes a quarterly newsletter 
to current city employees, retired employees and elected city 
Officials. 
A copy of this newsletter, known as the 
"Pipeline," is attached for your review. 
The newsletter on 
page eight contains a standard roster-type listing of elected 
officials .. 
The city of Torrance is requesting an advise letter from 
your office permitting the listing of elected officials in 
future newsletters. 
If you have any further questions please contact me. 
Thank you for your attention in this matter. 
Sincerely yours, 
Elizabeth G. Clark 
Sr. Deputy city Attorney

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Elizabeth G. Clark 
Sr. Deputy City Attorney 
3031 Torrance Boulevard 
Torrance, CA 
90503 
Dear Ms. Clark: 
September 9, 1988 
Re: 
88-352 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on September 7, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact John McLean, an attorney in the 
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329) .) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
Very truly yours,

VOLUME 7 NUMBER 1 
Dick Rankin 
He was the dark haired, tall fellow with 
glasses who you saw so often in the East 
Annex. You couid not work in the East 
Annex long without knowing him. Dick 
had a special gentleness that often goes 
with a big man. If he was in the hall, he 
was often answering a question for a 
citizen or City employee. You did not 
know Dick long before you knew how 
much UCLA meant to him. If you knew 
him well, you soon found a friend, a con-
fidant, and if you needed one, a confes-
sional. He had no guile. 
If you had a deadline, he would often 
stay until you both were exhausted, but 
the deadline would be met and the prod-
uct would be first class. 
And who among us who has had to write 
for Council or management cannot re-
member an evening in Dick's office, 
watching a loose group of ideas evolve 
Continued on page 7 
MARCH 1988 
Water, Water Everywhere 
Colorless, odorless, tasteless. 
It 
freezes solid at 32°F and vaporizes 
into the atmosphere at 212°F. it clus-
ters in the sky as clouds, falls as rain, 
collects in rivers, lakes, and basins, 
and gets pumped into your home so 
you can drink 8 glasses a day of it. 
Water. H20. Aqua Pura. Just how 
does the City go about the business of 
providing this liquid to consumers? 
The water comes from two sources. 
First there are Torrance's three wells, 
one of which is located at the Old Wa-
terYard at 1001 Elm Street. Until the 
1940's, all of the City's water came 
from these wells which, like other 
wells in this region, tap into the water 
table of the West Coast basin. How-
ever, increasing demand led local 
wells to be over-pumped causing sea-
Pumps still functioning in the pumping station 
at the Old Water Yard on Elm Street. 
Reservoir at the Old Water Yard on Elm Street. 
water to migrate in. While this saline 
intrusion threatened but did not affect 
Torrance wells, it is clear to see in this 
all-things-touch-all-other-things world 
that water is a very political substance. 
Water rights in the basin took 12 years of 
adjudication in the courts. Since then, 
the amou nt of wate r which Torrance and 
other cities and agencies can draw from 
their wells is legally limited by this adju-
dication. 
Consequently, today only 20% of the 
City's water comes from its three wells. 
The remaining 80% is bought from the 
Metropolitan Water District of Southern 
California, one of the world's largest 
water agencies, of which the City of Tor-
rance is a founding member. The Dis-
trict delivers water from the Colorado 
Continued on page 4
